network.overall_strategy: The network is centered on the literature-supported lactaldehyde -> threonine/allothreonine aminonitrile -> threonine module. Upstream convergence is built from (i) UV cyanosulfidic photoredox chemistry that converts glycolonitrile to glycolaldehyde and acetaldehyde cyanohydrin to lactaldehyde under CuCN/H2S/phosphate conditions, (ii) spark-discharge atmospheric/plume chemistry that supplies HCN, NH3, aldehydes, and directly low levels of threonine/allothreonine, (iii) HCN oligomerization to aminomalononitrile followed by aldehyde capture and hydrolysis as an alternative C-N assembly route, and (iv) hydrothermal redox-buffered Strecker-style feedstock generation and hydrolysis that can support aminonitrile formation if lactaldehyde is available, while acknowledging the instability of hydroxy amino acids at elevated temperature. Ten distinct pathways are represented as different connected routes through shared hubs. Key hubs are HCN, NH3, lactaldehyde, and threonine aminonitrile.

reactions.name: Volcanic-plume spark discharge generation of HCN and NH3 feedstocks

conditions.catalyst: TiO2 and sulfide mineral surfaces as atmospheric/aerosol deposition substrates
conditions.other: spark-discharge in reducing volcanic-plume style gas mixtures containing CH4, NH3 or N2 traces, H2S, CO2; rainout to surface waters

reactions.mechanism: Electrical discharge generates radical and ionic intermediates in reduced gas mixtures, producing cyanide and aldehydes that are deposited into surface settings.
reactions.reasoning: Spark-discharge experiments and their reanalysis show threonine-bearing product suites and imply formation of HCN, NH3, and aldehydes/ketones as precursor pools in volcanic-plume settings; Parker et al. 2011 and Ring et al. 1972 support this precursor-generating framework. Mineral agents are included here as surface deposition/photoactive minerals required by the schema, although the discharge chemistry itself is atmospheric rather than mineral-catalyzed. (Parker et al., 2011; Ring et al., 1972)

reactions.name: HCN hydration to formamide under UV-active surface conditions

conditions.other: UV-exposed evaporitic or geothermal pool; hydration and concentration during wet-dry cycling

reactions.mechanism: HCN is trapped and hydrated in sunlit surface waters to form a more persistent C-N reservoir that can feed downstream cyanide chemistry.
reactions.reasoning: The literature emphasizes cyanide concentration/conditioning and UV-active surface chemistry as part of cyanosulfidic prebiotic networks; this step is a conservative connector to hold cyanide in surface pools and is used here to connect atmospheric input to downstream HCN-derived chemistry. It is more weakly direct than the lactaldehyde module and is included as a necessary network-connecting step, explicitly marked as a systems-level extrapolation from cyanide handling rather than a threonine-specific demonstrated transformation. (Ritson & Sutherland, 2013; Green et al., 2021)

reactions.name: HCN-derived formation of glycolonitrile

conditions.other: UV-exposed aqueous pool with concentrated cyanide inventory

reactions.mechanism: HCN-derived C1/CN chemistry gives glycolonitrile, a canonical nitrile node entering cyanosulfidic photoredox reduction.
reactions.reasoning: Glycolonitrile is the explicit substrate for the first-stage Ritson-Sutherland photoreduction network and therefore must be included as a key upstream nitrile node. This reaction is included to connect the cyanide pool to that experimentally demonstrated substrate; the exact prebiotic route to glycolonitrile is not the threonine-specific focus of the report, so this connection is a necessary but somewhat schematic upstream condensation step. (Ritson & Sutherland, 2013; Green et al., 2021)

reactions.name: UV cyanosulfidic photoreduction of glycolonitrile to glycolaldehyde

conditions.catalyst: CuCN generated from sulfide mineral dissolution in cyanide solution; sodium phosphate buffer
conditions.other: 10 mM glycolonitrile, 30 mM H2S, 33 mM sodium phosphate, solid CuCN at 10 mol%, 254 nm irradiation, quartz vessel, 2 h; glycolaldehyde observed in 42% yield with acetaldehyde among aldehydic products

reactions.mechanism: Photoredox cycling of Cu(I) cyanide with sulfide-derived reducing equivalents produces hydrated-electron chemistry that reduces nitrile nodes to aldehydes.
reactions.reasoning: This is a directly reported threonine-relevant upstream reaction: glycolonitrile photoreduction under CuCN/H2S/phosphate/254 nm conditions yields glycolaldehyde and other aldehydes including acetaldehyde. The mineral connection via sulfide-derived CuCN generation is explicit in the literature. (Ritson & Sutherland, 2013; Green et al., 2021)

reactions.name: Cyanohydrin formation from acetaldehyde and HCN

conditions.other: ammonia-poor to moderate-ammonia aqueous settings favor cyanohydrin capture; wet-dry concentration can assist

reactions.mechanism: Nucleophilic addition of cyanide to acetaldehyde forms the corresponding cyanohydrin, the direct photoreduction substrate for lactaldehyde production.
reactions.reasoning: The deep research specifically identifies acetaldehyde cyanohydrin as the immediate photoredox precursor to lactaldehyde in the Ritson-Sutherland sequence and notes ammonia abundance as a branch control between cyanohydrins and aminonitriles. (Ritson & Sutherland, 2013; Kyushu meteoritic Strecker analysis)

reactions.name: UV cyanosulfidic photoreduction of acetaldehyde cyanohydrin to lactaldehyde

conditions.catalyst: CuCN generated from sulfide mineral dissolution in cyanide solution; phosphate buffer
conditions.other: 254 nm irradiation for 2 h; lactaldehyde reported in 19% yield from acetaldehyde cyanohydrin photoreduction

reactions.mechanism: CuCN-mediated UV photoredox reduction converts the cyanohydrin node into the aldehyde-level Strecker precursor lactaldehyde.
reactions.reasoning: This is the key literature-defined dedicated threonine carbonyl-precursor step: second-stage cyanohydrin photoreduction gives lactaldehyde, explicitly identified as the precursor of (allo-)threonine. (Ritson & Sutherland, 2013; Green et al., 2021)

reactions.name: HCN oligomerization to aminomalononitrile

conditions.other: concentrating pools with repeated evaporation and rehydration; oligomerization competes with polymerization

reactions.mechanism: Concentrated HCN undergoes oligomerization to multifunctional aminonitrile-rich species, including aminomalononitrile.
reactions.reasoning: Aminomalononitrile is a key HCN oligomer discussed as an alternative C-N assembly route to threonine/allothreonine after reaction with electrophiles and hydrolysis. The literature notes plausibility alongside selectivity and accumulation challenges. (Thanassi, 1975; Green et al., 2021)

reactions.name: Strecker assembly from lactaldehyde to threonine aminonitrile

conditions.other: addition of HCN and NH3 to photoreduction products; aminonitrile formation over days to weeks; via imine/cyanohydrin-mediated Strecker sequence

reactions.mechanism: Lactaldehyde reacts with ammonia to form an imine/iminium equivalent, followed by cyanide addition to give the threonine/allothreonine aminonitrile mixture.
reactions.reasoning: This is the core module supported across the report: lactaldehyde is explicitly the carbonyl precursor for threonine/allothreonine, and Ritson-Sutherland report smooth conversion to the Strecker intermediate aminonitrile on addition of HCN and NH3. (Ritson & Sutherland, 2013; Green et al., 2021; Kyushu meteoritic Strecker analysis)

reactions.name: AMN-electrophile route: aminomalononitrile capture of lactaldehyde to threonine-family nitrile precursor

conditions.other: mild aqueous conditions; aldehyde electrophile capture followed by network-internal hydrolytic rearrangement to threonine-family aminonitrile precursor

reactions.mechanism: Aminomalononitrile acts as a multifunctional C-N synthon, adding to aldehydic electrophiles and channeling carbon and nitrogen into threonine/allothreonine precursor space through a route distinct from canonical one-carbonyl Strecker assembly.
reactions.reasoning: This reaction is intentionally separated from the canonical Strecker step to address the prior compression problem. Thanassi 1975 supports AMN plus aldehydes leading, after hydrolysis, to threonine/allothreonine, but the detailed intermediate structure is less explicitly resolved than for the lactaldehyde Strecker branch. Therefore this node is represented as converging on the experimentally relevant threonine-family aminonitrile space while acknowledging partial mechanistic compression. This is literature-supported but less specific than rxn_008. (Thanassi, 1975)

reactions.name: Hydrolysis of threonine aminonitrile to threonine and allothreonine

conditions.other: prolonged aqueous hydrolysis in prebiotic pools or during wet-dry/geothermal cycling; expected to yield diastereomeric threonine and allothreonine

reactions.mechanism: Aminonitrile hydrolysis proceeds through amide/carboxyl equivalents to the alpha-amino acid products, preserving the expected threonine/allothreonine diastereomeric outcome.
reactions.reasoning: Hydrolysis of aminonitriles to alpha-amino acids is the standard terminal step of Strecker-type synthesis and is explicitly framed in meteoritic Strecker discussions. The report also emphasizes that abiotic syntheses typically generate threonine together with allothreonine. (Kyushu meteoritic Strecker analysis; Ritson & Sutherland, 2013; Ring et al., 1972)

reactions.name: Direct spark-discharge production of threonine and allothreonine

conditions.catalyst: TiO2 and sulfide mineral deposition surfaces
conditions.other: reducing or locally reducing discharge system; product residues accumulate and wash into surface waters; threonine present at low abundance relative to glycine and largely racemic

reactions.mechanism: Complex atmospheric radical chemistry followed by aqueous post-processing yields a suite of amino acids directly, including threonine and allothreonine.
reactions.reasoning: Both Parker et al. 2011 and Ring et al. 1972 report threonine in spark-discharge residues, with Ring et al. explicitly confirming both threonine and allothreonine. This direct route is included as an experimentally grounded alternative path to the target, while also returning HCN and aldehyde feedstocks to the connected network. Mineral agents are again interpreted as environmental deposition/concentration surfaces to satisfy the schema. (Parker et al., 2011; Ring et al., 1972)

reactions.name: Hydrothermal redox-buffered Strecker-style support and hydrolysis route to threonine from imported lactaldehyde

conditions.catalyst: pyrite-pyrrhotite-magnetite style redox buffer represented here by pyrite, magnetite, and FeS/mackinawite
conditions.other: hydrothermal Strecker-type conditions modeled on HCN/aldehyde/NH4+ experiments; included as a constrained route because threonine itself was not explicitly reported and hydroxy amino acids may degrade

reactions.mechanism: If lactaldehyde is delivered into hydrothermal flank settings, redox-buffered Strecker aminonitrile formation and partial hydrolysis can occur, but product persistence is limited by hydrothermal degradation of hydroxy amino acids.
reactions.reasoning: Hydrothermal Strecker chemistry is experimentally documented for several amino acids under 150 C and 10 bar with pyrite-pyrrhotite-magnetite buffers, but threonine was not explicitly tabulated; therefore this route is included only as a literature-constrained extension requiring imported lactaldehyde and explicitly acknowledging instability/degradation of hydroxy amino acids. It should be regarded as plausible but less directly demonstrated than the surface lactaldehyde route. (Andersson & Holm, 2000 summarizing Hennet et al., 1992; Bada et al., 1978; Keefe et al., 1995)

pathways.name: Canonical cyanosulfidic threonine pathway from atmospheric cyanide
pathways.description: Spark-discharge feedstocks supply cyanide and acetaldehyde; acetaldehyde cyanohydrin is photoreduced to lactaldehyde, then Strecker assembly gives threonine aminonitrile and hydrolysis gives threonine.

pathways.name: Glycolonitrile-first photoredox pathway
pathways.description: Atmospheric cyanide is converted to glycolonitrile, photoreduced to glycolaldehyde plus acetaldehyde, then passes through cyanohydrin and lactaldehyde to threonine aminonitrile and threonine.

pathways.name: Direct spark-discharge threonine synthesis
pathways.description: High-energy atmospheric discharge directly generates threonine and allothreonine in complex amino-acid mixtures.

pathways.name: AMN alternative route from atmospheric cyanide to threonine
pathways.description: Atmospheric cyanide oligomerizes to aminomalononitrile; independently generated lactaldehyde is captured by AMN-derived chemistry and hydrolyzed to threonine.

pathways.name: Hybrid discharge-photoredox route via direct acetaldehyde supply
pathways.description: Acetaldehyde from discharge chemistry enters the cyanohydrin-photoreduction sequence without requiring the glycolonitrile branch.

pathways.name: Hybrid discharge-photoredox route with HCN conditioning through formamide
pathways.description: HCN is held in surface reservoirs as formamide-linked cyanide chemistry before entering glycolonitrile photoredox chemistry and then the lactaldehyde Strecker module.

pathways.name: HCN oligomer route merged with canonical lactaldehyde module
pathways.description: An HCN-rich pool simultaneously supports AMN formation and the canonical lactaldehyde Strecker route, yielding threonine through an alternative C-N assembly entry point.

pathways.name: Hydrothermal completion from surface-generated lactaldehyde
pathways.description: Surface photochemistry generates lactaldehyde, which is transported into hydrothermal flank settings where redox-buffered Strecker chemistry and hydrolysis can produce threonine, though with degradation risk.

pathways.name: Glycolonitrile-to-hydrothermal hybrid pathway
pathways.description: The full surface glycolonitrile photoredox sequence generates lactaldehyde, which then feeds a hydrothermal redox-buffered Strecker/hydrolysis step.

pathways.name: Surface network with parallel direct and indirect threonine production
pathways.description: A single plume-fed environment can yield some threonine directly by discharge while also building a larger threonine pool indirectly through the lactaldehyde aminonitrile route.

pathway_evaluations.feedback: Strong literature-aligned route. The acetaldehyde cyanohydrin to lactaldehyde photoreduction and subsequent Strecker conversion to threonine aminonitrile closely match the Ritson-Sutherland framework, and hydrolysis to threonine/allothreonine is chemically standard. The main limitation is that the atmospheric discharge step compresses many feedstock-generating processes into one reaction and likely overstates direct co-generation of NH3, HCN, and acetaldehyde in a single clean step.
suggestions: Specify CuCN/phosphate/254 nm UV explicitly for the cyanosulfidic steps
Clarify whether aminonitrile hydrolysis is neutral, acidic, or geothermally assisted
Separate atmospheric feedstock generation from aqueous cyanohydrin formation for better mechanistic resolution

pathway_evaluations.feedback: This pathway captures the intended cyanosulfidic upstream logic, but the HCN to formamide to glycolonitrile sequence is weakly specified and not a well-supported direct route in the cited threonine literature. The downstream glycolonitrile photoreduction and lactaldehyde module are plausible, but the conditioning segment reduces confidence.
suggestions: Replace the formamide-conditioned route with a more direct HCN plus formaldehyde route to glycolonitrile if formaldehyde is included as a node
Add explicit literature-supported conditions for glycolonitrile formation
Avoid implying formamide is a necessary precursor to glycolonitrile without stronger support

pathway_evaluations.feedback: Direct spark-discharge production of threonine/allothreonine is well supported by classic and reanalyzed Miller-type experiments. However, as a one-step pathway it is chemically opaque and does not show intermediate-level mechanism or environmental handoff. It is credible as an empirical production route, but weak as a mechanistically explicit synthesis pathway.
suggestions: Frame this explicitly as empirical complex-mixture synthesis rather than a mechanistically resolved one-step conversion
Add washout or aqueous post-discharge maturation if the pathway is to be integrated more realistically with the rest of the network

pathway_evaluations.feedback: Good alternative route grounded in Thanassi-style AMN chemistry. HCN oligomerization to AMN is plausible, and AMN plus aldehyde capture followed by hydrolysis to threonine/allothreonine is supported in principle. The main uncertainty is that rxn_009 compresses multiple transformations and the exact nitrile-level intermediate is underspecified.
suggestions: Represent the AMN-derived adduct as a distinct intermediate before hydrolysis
Clarify whether lactaldehyde is still required stoichiometrically or whether other aldehydes can branch into the same family

pathway_evaluations.feedback: This hybrid route is effectively a strong variant of the canonical photoredox-Strecker pathway, with direct atmospheric acetaldehyde supply reducing dependence on more speculative upstream chemistry. It is among the best routes in the network.
suggestions: Note that atmospheric acetaldehyde supply is plausible but may be low-yield and episodic
Include compartmentalization or evaporative concentration to support Strecker completion

pathway_evaluations.feedback: As with P2, the weakness is not in the lactaldehyde-to-threonine module but in the formamide-conditioned HCN route to glycolonitrile. The later steps are solid; the front-end chemistry is insufficiently supported in the supplied literature.
suggestions: Remove the formamide detour unless independently justified
Use better-established cyanide-aldehyde chemistry to reach glycolonitrile

pathway_evaluations.feedback: This is a plausible merged pathway joining AMN chemistry with the lactaldehyde module. It benefits from literature support for both components, but the merger step remains structurally compressed and would be stronger with an explicit AMN-lactaldehyde adduct node.
suggestions: Introduce the AMN-derived precursor explicitly to improve mechanistic clarity
Specify hydrolysis conditions after AMN addition chemistry

pathway_evaluations.feedback: The network correctly treats hydrothermal chemistry as a support/completion route rather than the primary origin of threonine, but rxn_012 overstates hydrothermal confidence. Literature supports hydrothermal Strecker chemistry for some amino acids under mineral-buffered conditions, yet threonine specifically is not established and hydroxy amino acid instability at elevated temperature is a serious concern.
suggestions: Downgrade the hydrothermal route to a speculative support pathway
State explicit mineral buffers and conditions if retained
Emphasize possible degradation of threonine/allothreonine under hydrothermal residence

pathway_evaluations.feedback: This hybrid suffers from both major weak points in the network: the under-supported formamide-to-glycolonitrile segment and the overstated hydrothermal completion step. Although connected, it is among the least convincing routes chemically.
suggestions: Replace the upstream conditioning chemistry
Avoid hydrothermal completion unless framed as low-confidence
Keep the surface photoredox-Strecker sequence as the dominant route

pathway_evaluations.feedback: A strong integrated surface network. Parallel direct spark-discharge production and indirect photoredox-Strecker production are mutually reinforcing and consistent with the literature emphasis that threonine can arise both as a direct component of discharge mixtures and via a more explicit lactaldehyde-centered pathway.
suggestions: Distinguish empirical direct synthesis from mechanistically resolved indirect synthesis in the pathway description
Include geochemical concentration/holding steps to better justify downstream aminonitrile accumulation

evaluation.network_feedback: Overall, this is a good and mostly literature-aligned threonine synthesis network. Its strongest feature is the correct identification of lactaldehyde and threonine aminonitrile as central hubs, matching the deep research findings. The network also appropriately includes direct spark-discharge production, cyanosulfidic photoredox generation of lactaldehyde from acetaldehyde cyanohydrin, and an AMN-based alternative route. Topologically, it is well connected, has multiple convergent routes, and achieves the target repeatedly. The biggest weaknesses are structural and compliance-related: the network claims a hydrothermal component but provides no mineral agents for the hydrothermal or surface reactions despite the required schema, and several surface steps are underspecified relative to the literature, especially the HCN to formamide to glycolonitrile segment. The hydrothermal route to threonine is weakly supported by the supplied literature and should be treated as speculative due to poor direct evidence for threonine formation and likely degradation of hydroxy amino acids at elevated temperature. Literature alignment is otherwise strong in concept, but because reaction-level citations and exact conditions are absent from the presented network, it falls short of full compliance with the requested synthesis-network standard.
